title: 2 Corinthians
author: Paul, the apostle, with Timothy, from Macedonia
setting: Paul writes after a painful visit to Corinth and a severe letter (now lost). A portion of the church has been reconciled to him; others follow 'super-apostles' who challenge his authority. The letter is the most autobiographical of Paul's letters.
purpose: To defend the authentic apostolic ministry (suffering, weakness, and the power of Christ) against the polished self-promoters, to complete the collection for Jerusalem's poor, and to reaffirm the glory of the new covenant.
themes: Strength through weakness — God's power in frailty
The new covenant and its surpassing glory
The suffering apostle as a living sermon
Generosity as grace — the Jerusalem collection
The thorn in the flesh: 'my grace is sufficient'

outline.label: Paul's Ministry of Comfort and Reconciliation

before.label: 1 Corinthians — divisions, disorders, and the cross as answer
Paul's painful visit fails; a severe letter written
Timothy reports the Corinthians' repentance to Paul

after.label: Paul's third visit to Corinth to complete the collection
Romans — written from Corinth at the end of this journey
Jerusalem journey with the Gentile collection; Paul arrested

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: Writing the most personally revealing of his letters, Paul defends his apostolic ministry against 'super-apostles' who have challenged his authority, and pours out his own suffering and weakness as evidence of genuine apostleship

key_people.name: Titus
key_people.role: Paul's co-worker and emissary who brought good news of the Corinthians' repentance (7:6-7) and is being sent again with this letter to complete the collection for Jerusalem

context: 2 Corinthians was written c. AD 55-57 from Macedonia, after a painful visit to Corinth and a severe letter that preceded this one. The church's response to the severe letter was largely positive, but a group of opponents ('super-apostles', 11:5) were still undermining Paul's authority by contrasting his weak physical presence and refusal of payment with what they claimed was genuine apostolic power and status. The letter is Paul's most personal and his most sustained reflection on the nature of apostolic ministry.
christ_connection: The paradox of Paul's ministry — power through weakness, life through death — mirrors and participates in the pattern of Christ's own ministry. The 'treasure in jars of clay' passage (4:7-12) makes this explicit: the extraordinary power of the gospel is revealed precisely through the apostle's weakness, so that it is clearly God's power and not human achievement. The theology of the cross is not just Paul's message but the form of his ministry: 'always carrying in the body the death of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may also be manifested in our bodies' (4:10). The new covenant ministry Paul describes in ch. 3 is the ministry of the Spirit who transforms believers into the image of Christ.

key_verses.note: 'We have this treasure in jars of clay, to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us' — the theology of weakness and power that governs Paul's entire apostolic self-understanding
'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' — the most concentrated NT statement of imputation and substitutionary exchange
'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness' — the Lord's direct answer to Paul's thorn in the flesh, paradigmatic for all Christian suffering

themes_detail.title: The Theology of Weakness and Power
themes_detail.text: 2 Corinthians is Paul's sustained argument that the pattern of Christ's death and resurrection — weakness that becomes the vehicle of divine power — defines authentic apostolic ministry. His catalogue of sufferings (4:8-9; 6:4-10; 11:23-29) is not self-pity but theological demonstration: these sufferings are evidence of genuine apostleship, not its refutation. The super-apostles who impress Corinth with their eloquence and social status are measuring ministry by the wrong standard. The 'thorn in the flesh' episode (12:7-10) is the most personal expression of this theology: God's deliberate refusal to remove weakness so that his power might be displayed through it.

themes_detail.title: The Ministry of the New Covenant
themes_detail.text: Chapters 3-4 contain Paul's most developed comparison of old and new covenants. The Mosaic ministry, though glorious, was a ministry of condemnation and death — the law exposed sin but could not remove it. The new covenant ministry is a ministry of the Spirit that gives life, of righteousness, and whose glory is surpassing and permanent. The veil over Moses' face (Exod 34:33-35) was to hide the fading glory; the veil over Israel's heart (3:14-15) prevents them from seeing Christ in their own scriptures. The Spirit lifts this veil, and beholding Christ's glory transforms believers progressively into his image (3:18).

themes_detail.title: Reconciliation as the Gospel's Content and Paul's Ministry
themes_detail.text: 2 Corinthians 5:14-21 contains one of the NT's most compressed accounts of the atonement: Christ died for all so that those who live might live for him; God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself; he made Christ to be sin so that we might become God's righteousness. The theological content (reconciliation) and its ministerial application (Paul's own ministry of reconciliation) are inseparable — Paul does not just preach about reconciliation but enacts it as an ambassador, making an appeal on Christ's behalf: 'be reconciled to God.' The gospel is both an announcement and an invitation that requires a personal response.

themes_detail.title: Generosity as Participation in the Grace of Christ
themes_detail.text: Chapters 8-9 address the collection for the Jerusalem poor with a sustained theology of generous giving. The foundation is christological: 'you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (8:9). Generosity is not fundraising strategy but participation in the self-giving pattern of Christ's incarnation. The principle of equality (8:13-14) envisions the church as a community of mutual provision; the promise of blessing for the generous giver (9:6-15) is grounded in God's own inexhaustible generosity — 'Thanks be to God for his inexpressible gift' (9:15).
